sentences.text: Yeah.
That's us.
And like I-- they had a few German civilians working there.
Of war.
You know-- [LAUGHS]
Young people, right?
What can I tell you?
I don't know how we even communicated.
I certainly-- to this day, I don't know French, and I don't care.
There was a couple woman-man interaction, believe it or not.
Well, I don't know that it went that far, because there was just no place to do-- [LAUGHS]-- to do it.
But the feelings-- the feelings were there, absolutely.
Feelings were there.
Terrible in that--
Yeah, [?
Haza. ?]
I think triple bunks we had at that point already.
I can't tell you this, because they kept constantly sending us people.
As a matter of fact--
No, alone-- alone-- on a straw--
Mattress, yeah.
That's-- that's what you-- that's what I had.
I had-- oh-- postcards that were written by my sister and my father.
And--
No.
No.
And this picture of my sister and my niece and her friend.
I did hide them when I knew that we were going to have an inspection, because we weren't allowed to have anything like this.
Well, we never knew, you know.
We'd like find out in the last minute.
Now, I wouldn't say days a week.
I don't know, maybe a couple, few times a month.
It depends, you know, whether they were just talking to the commandant or whether they wanted to see the--
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
6 o'clock in the morning.
Well, snow, rain, heat, whatever, you stood out there, and they counted.
And if they were missing, they counted again.
And if they were missing, they counted again.
Oh, sometimes an hour, hour and a half.
Oh, and by then, we wore sort of a uniform already.
It was like a-- made like of a material like a sack.
And it was like a wrap-around thing that had a hole on the side where you put a thing and-- like an apron.
And you tied it in the back.
Yeah.
You know what they called us?
Political prisons.
That's the way they had us--
Yeah.
Because we had the red stripe, and then a yellow triangle.
And I think we had the number over it.
But the red stripe meant political.
Not that-- not that I remember.
No, but we got a whole bunch of Hungarian Jewish girls.
But that was later on.
That was later on.
Well, if God helped you, that you got well on your own, you were OK.
At the first one.
Then-- it wasn't an infirmary.
But like I said, she'd go out and get medication, you know.
Oh, I think we did have a doctor coming in occasionally.
But not at [?
Haza ?].
Haza ?] was just-- forget it.
Yes.
We all did.
Yeah, we all did.
About home, about the people we used to know at-- you know, from--
Because most of us were more or less from the same-- not-- not necessarily from the city, but around there.
Yeah.
Well, we tired.
The best we could.
If somebody got sick, if you-- if you could share some-- maybe some food or some-- which, believe me, it didn't happen too often.
Because I think the human nature is 'I'm first.' That's what I-- I witnessed it.
But that's later on.
Yeah.
No.
But I'll tell you one thing that I had witnessed with the Germans, with the Nazis, with the big shots, the SS, the Gestapo.
The civilian people had no jurisdiction over us.
They were not allowed into the camp-- only the ones in uniform.
Do you understand what I'm--
Yes, yes.
So it was a military--
So in other words, we-- we didn't have any fears that something could happen to us from the people on the outside, you know.
Well, who knows what they were capable?
They were capable of doing anything and everything.
We were afraid of everything.
I'm still afraid of a lot of things, believe me.
Yes.
I had a relationship with a man that-- he was a mechanic.
And he was fixing the machines when something went wrong.
I think he was a-- what they call a Social Democrat in those days.
And he really-- he was-- he really took his life in his own hands, because he was so good to us.
He-- he, you know, got to know a few of us.
And you-- you can't trust-- I mean, we wouldn't have squealed, but some people just-- I don't know.
They can-- it's not that you can't trust them, but they can say things that they may be sorry for, you know what I'm-- And he would get behind the machine, where he-- nobody could see him.
And he would speak to us.
He would be telling us how the war's, you know, going.
And he would try and bring us some food.
And then if he heard that somebody was sick and needed a medication, he tried to get it.
We loved him.
I forgot.
But you know what we called him?
We couldn't refer to him by name.
Because if we were overhead, you know, he would have been-- we called him Uncle.
But we called him in Polish.
And in Polish, 'uncle' is 'wujek.' But we gave him an endearing-- Vuichu, which is a-- and that's how we referred to him.
Well, he outlived his years.
And, you know, he was an older man.
But he was-- he was really something.
He was really and truly something.
Oh, yeah.
I can't remember names.
Oh, yeah, constantly.
That was the-- that was the good one yet.
Himmler was there.
Yeah.
I think just that one time.
Yeah.
No.
Just stand.
Nothing.
He didn't talk to us.
He looked-- you know, looked around, looked at the-- whatever.
Well, that's what-- that's where Vuichu came in, because he kept giving us reports.
Because he was listening to the British stations.
You know something?
Not really.
Because I think-- well, in the Sudetenland, where we were-- I don't know.
I don't think we were anywhere too close to railroad tracks or anything like this.
It was strictly factories, all textile factories.
But we knew that, you know-- that Hitler was losing.
We just didn't know how long and what-- how long it would take.
And then like girls got sick.
They used to have-- we used to have selections in our camp.
They  used to send them to Auschwitz.
We knew that.
Yes.
By then, I think we knew already.
Now, maybe not exactly how and what they-- how they exterminated.
But we knew Auschwitz.
We knew the word Auschwitz.

And everybody tried to-- the best you could to stay-- stay-- if you stayed well enough to do your work and survive on what they gave you, survive the beatings, survive the-- the things, the hangings, the shootings, whatever they did to the other people, they left you alone.
Not to an extent.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I got my licks.
Well, most-- the one in the first camp, I am so scared of doctors to this day that I cannot begin to tell you.
That examination, supposedly, that physical-- that was so vivid in my mind that I see myself standing there now and them looking at me and telling me to turn around.
And on that basis, they decided whether I should live or die.
And I have such fear of doctors.
Yeah.
To this day.
I have a lot of fears.
I have a tremendous fear of authority.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yeah.
At times, we didn't think we were going to make it.
I don't know.
I don't know whether the will, the will to live-- but I think deep down, we all knew already by 1944 that we had nothing to go home to.
I'm not saying that everybody-- some people did have family left.
But what is very much outstanding in my mind-- 1945, when the-- at the beginning of 1945 when the Russians were advancing, the Germans were sending the Jews from Auschwitz.
They were sending them-- the marches into Germany.
And one transport-- all men-- it was the first time I saw a Jewish man since 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They came into our camp-- not for their benefit, because the SS marched them-- with them.
And they needed a rest.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I have never seen-- [SOB]-- we looked next to them like-- like we just got back from a resort or something.
And we tried to help them, you know, give them whatever we had.
And we didn't have a lot, believe me.
We didn't have enough for ourselves.
But when we saw them, they were emaciated.
They couldn't walk.
And we saw-- the first time I saw the uniforms that they wore, the stripes.
And we got orders-- you are not to help these people.
Anybody caught will get shot.
They did shoot a few people.
Because we just-- and I-- I have never seen-- they were-- the prisoners, they were killing each other to catch a piece of bread.
A couple of days, until the SS rested up.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
I was-- till the liberation.
May 9.
Well, I've got to tell you something very, very interesting.
See, they ran out of the raw material.
Because Russia was moving and taking back their territory.
We had nothing to work with.
Excuse me.
We had nothing to work with.
And they couldn't see us sitting idle in the camps.
They took us out to dig ditches in an open field.
No purpose-- just so we should do something.
And we were-- only women-- SS women.
And I'm telling you, they were worse than men.
And we're standing there.
We're digging with shovels.
We're digging.
We don't know what we're digging, but we're digging.
And we were watched.
All of a sudden, we hear planes.
We were in an open field.
So how can you not, you know, lift your head and look?
And we couldn't believe what we saw-- leaflets-- millions-- coming down.
Right away, we hear [GERMAN].
Anybody picking up-- I spoke German, believe it or not.
I don't speak it anymore.
Anybody picking up one of those gets shot on the spot.
But you know something?
So many came down that you couldn't help it.
Because they  were all over you.
We got a few into camp.
And this was, I think, about a week or two weeks before the end of the war.
What did the leaflet say?
OK.
It was addressed to the German people.
I don't remember-- I wish I had held onto one.
I wish that I had held onto one.
You killed enough.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Stop your killing.
You can't bring back the ones you killed.
Stop your killing now.
Because you are really going to suffer consequences and whatever.
And it was signed Roosevelt, Stalin, and Churchill.
That's when we knew that-- oh, how I wish I had-- but you know, who thinks, you know, at a time like this?
So you felt the end was coming near?
Yeah.
And then, I think, about a week later-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] get up one morning, and we don't see any SS women-- nobody.
And this man, this Vuichu, the German soldiers were retreating with their guns and whatever.
And we were, by that time, I would say, at least 2,000 girls in that camp.
Because they brought in a huge amount of the Hungarian in 1944, end of 1944 when Eichmann came into Hungary.
And funny thing is, I think they brought him into us from Auschwitz.
Because they came with their hair shaved off.
See, we always had our hair.
Unfortunately, it wasn't always clean, believe me.
And we were all young women.
And we were afraid to be there alone.
Like I said, they-- no civilian had any jurisdiction over us as long as the Nazis, the SS, was in control.
About three days.
Without anyone.
So he got a couple of his friends, and they had guns.
And they stood at the gate of our camp.
And they guarded us.
How about food?
What did you do for food at that time?
You know, I don't remember.
I just don't remember.
Were the townspeople so antagonistic towards you that you were afraid of them?
The who?
The Germans?
The--
The people that lived in the area.
It wasn't so much even the people that lived in the area.
It was the soldiers.
They were drunk.
They were retreating, you know.
And--
You mean the German soldiers--
Solider.
Right.
Right.
Russians.
I'll tell you what I remember.
It must have been about 4 o'clock in the afternoon on May 9.
The war was officially over May 8.
And Vuichu came around, and he got a few of us together.
He didn't have any children.
And he obviously talked to his wife about us all through-- over the years.
He says, my wife made dinner for you.
He says, you're coming with me to my house.
Yeah.
They were beginning to-- yeah, to walk out.
So-- I don't know-- four, five of us.
I can't remember.
And we went to his house.
And she made-- we were walking.
And as we were walking, we heard like a roar-- a roar-- noise, noise, noise.
The Russians were coming in with the tanks.
And of course, we stopped, and we knew-- and we all knew one   Russian word, [RUSSIAN], which is hello, you know.
And-- we wore our Star of David.
Oh, yeah.
We-- of course.
I don't remember whether we-- I had the-- maybe I had taken it off and put it on, something else, whatever I had.
I don't remember.
But we wanted them to know that we're Jewish.
And they came in, and they-- they occupied.
Oh, they came into the camps.
See, we were at his house.
And this woman saved us some chocolate.
She knew that we wouldn't-- we didn't have it all these years.
So she saved us some chocolate.
We came back to camp.
And I want to tell you something.
The Russians brought in a lot of food and booze, you know.
They were known for that.
And the girls were having a ball-- a party.
Which prisoners?
Oh, you mean us girls?
Oh, no.
They were very good to us.
Yeah.
Because they have-- they had a reputation of rape.
Yeah, a couple of-- there were a couple of incidents.
Yes, in the upper-- the officers.
Some, yeah.
We weren't that-- like you see the other people.
Like I said, if you did what they wanted, the work that they gave you, and you could live on what they gave you-- and it also depends on one's makeup or whatever.
No, we-- overall, we didn't look too bad.
We didn't look like some of these people that came out of the camps.
No.
I stayed in the camp.
We stayed in the camp.
But Czech government took over, you know.
They came in-- back to power.
And they decided they didn't want us there.
Go back home where you came from.
And they provided us with transportation.
And went back to Poland.
About a month after.

Because, you know, it was also that the Russians were saying to the girls, take us anyplace you want to, anybody's apartment, and take anything you want-- jewelry, furs, or whatever-- which was OK to go into Germans' homes, but not to Czech homes.
Because they suffered, too, you know.
They suffered, too.
So they got a little fed up with us.
They ran away.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Some of them, they caught.
And the girls dealt with them.
Shaved their heads, beat them up, maybe killed one or two.
They did.
They did.
There was retaliation, definitely.
Yeah.
It took me a week on the cattle trains.
Because the-- it was chaos-- chaos-- traveling.
I don't remember.
I think they gave us some.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] I think they gave us some.
I can't remember.
I came back.
I went back to Sosnowiec.
And at that time, they had already established a-- a committee, a Jewish committee.
Because, I-- well, first of all, I was liberated a day later than anybody else, which wasn't really that much of a difference.
And--
What did I find?
I went to my home, my apartment.
Yeah, by Pollocks.
And Hitler didn't kill you yet?
Yeah.
That was the reaction.
Hitler didn't kill you?
I knew I'm not going to live there.
I knew it.
So we went together, a few girls that we-- you know, a group that we stuck together over the years.
And I don't know how we got an apartment, a room, or something.
And we moved in.
I don't even know how we lived.
Maybe we got money from the committee, the Jewish committee.
I really don't remember.
No.
And we--
Well, so I came into this committee.
I registered.
And I wanted to know, did anybody-- stop.
Not at that point.
About six weeks-- between four and six weeks.
And a whole bunch of us picked ourselves up and, ironically, went back to Germany.
Because all the action was there.
